texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. Introduce several KAM theorems for infinite dimensional Hamiltonian with short range and discuss the relationship between spectra of linearized operator and invariant tori. Especially, introduce a KAM theorem in the paper[21] published in CMP 2002, which shows that there are rich KAM tori for a class of Hamiltonian with short range and with linearized operator of pure point spectra. Here are also presented several open problems.
texts.text: Abstract. Introduce several KAM theorems for infinite dimensional Hamiltonian with short range and discuss the relationship between spectra of linearized operator and invariant tori. Especially, introduce a KAM theorem in the paper[21] published in CMP 2002, which shows that there are rich KAM tori for a class of Hamiltonian with short range and with linearized operator of pure point spectra. Here are also presented several open problems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study of the finite dimensional KAM tori for infinite dimensional Hamiltonian defined by partial differential equations was initiated by Kuksin in the end of 1980's. There are now a lot of work on this field. Here we focus on the Hamiltonian of short range instead of PDEs.
texts.text: The study of the finite dimensional KAM tori for infinite dimensional Hamiltonian defined by partial differential equations was initiated by Kuksin in the end of 1980's. There are now a lot of work on this field. Here we focus on the Hamiltonian of short range instead of PDEs.

[Yuan[21],2002] Suppose that the Hamiltonian (2.1) satisfies conditions (A1-4).Then, for a given γ > 0 there is a small constant ε ∗ = ε ∗ ( Π , N , γ , δ a ) > 0 such that, if 0 < ε < ε ∗ ,then there is a Cantor set Π∞ ⊂ Π with meas Π∞ ≥ ( meas Π ) · ( 1 -O ( γ )) ,an analytic family of torus embedding Ψ∞ : T N × Π → P ,and a map ω∞ : Π → R N ,such that for each ξ ∈ Π∞ ,the map Ψ∞ restricted to T N ×{ ξ } is an analytic embedding of rotational torus with frequencies ω∞ ( ξ ) for the Hamiltonian H.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: for example. In the classical case breathers are time-periodic and spatially localized solutions of the equations of motion. Aubry [1]-[4] posed the well-known concept of anti-integrability or anticontinuation by which the existence of the breathers for some equations of motion can be proven. While searching periodic breathers, small divisor problem does not appear. Thus, in 1994, the existence of periodic breathers in a wide class of models (including (2.6) was proved by MacKay and Aubry [17], using the anti-integrability. Naturally, one hopes to investigate so-called quasi-periodic breathers, i.e., the solutions which are quasi-periodic in time and exponential decay (localization) in space. When searching quasi-periodic breathers, here indeed appears the small divisor problem. Thus, Aubry[2] remarked that the existence of the quasi-periodic breathers is an open problem which should relate the concepts of the KAM theory and of anti-integrability. By using Theorem 2, the open problem was solved with the assumption that the linearized system has no continuous spectrum.
texts.text: for example. In the classical case breathers are time-periodic and spatially localized solutions of the equations of motion. Aubry [1]-[4] posed the well-known concept of anti-integrability or anticontinuation by which the existence of the breathers for some equations of motion can be proven. While searching periodic breathers, small divisor problem does not appear. Thus, in 1994, the existence of periodic breathers in a wide class of models (including (2.6) was proved by MacKay and Aubry [17], using the anti-integrability. Naturally, one hopes to investigate so-called quasi-periodic breathers, i.e., the solutions which are quasi-periodic in time and exponential decay (localization) in space. When searching quasi-periodic breathers, here indeed appears the small divisor problem. Thus, Aubry[2] remarked that the existence of the quasi-periodic breathers is an open problem which should relate the concepts of the KAM theory and of anti-integrability. By using Theorem 2, the open problem was solved with the assumption that the linearized system has no continuous spectrum.

texts.orig: which implies that, in this case, the frequencies λ n 's ( n ∈ Z ) are the most 'dense', i.e., they are condensed at a point β . The main aim of Theorem 2 is to prove the existence of quasi-periodic breathers for (2.6). Therefore, we take λ n = β in Theorem 2. Actually, it is not necessary to impose any conditions on the frequencies λ n , especially not necessary to assume that all frequencies λ n are the same: λ n = β . In fact, the scheme of KAM iteration in the proof of Theorem 2 is the same as that devised by Kuksin[14] for partial differential equations. In the both cases, one encounters the following small divisor problem:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3. We have seen that the pure point spectra property is of key importance for the existence of quasi-periodic breathers for Hamiltonian with short range. This property is fulfilled by assume W ( x ) = O ( | x | 3 ) in Theorem 3. If W ( x ) = 1 2 x 2 + · · · , the linearized system of (2.6) is of continuous spectra which is the whole of the interval [ β , β + 1 ] . When searching periodic breathers, there is no small divisors. Thus, in Aubry-MacKay's work[17], the coupled potential W may be O ( | x | 2 ) , which involving continuous spectra. If searching quasi-periodic solution of some symmetry, small divisors can also be avoided. Consequently, in 2002, Bambusi and Vella [5] found many (but with measure 0) quasi-periodic breathers of some symmetry for W ( x ) = cx 2 + O ( x 3 ) .
texts.text: Remark 3. We have seen that the pure point spectra property is of key importance for the existence of quasi-periodic breathers for Hamiltonian with short range. This property is fulfilled by assume W ( x ) = O ( | x | 3 ) in Theorem 3. When searching periodic breathers, there is no small divisors. Thus, in Aubry-MacKay's work[17], the coupled potential W may be O ( | x | 2 ) , which involving continuous spectra. If searching quasi-periodic solution of some symmetry, small divisors can also be avoided. Consequently, in 2002, Bambusi and Vella [5] found many (but with measure 0) quasi-periodic breathers of some symmetry for W ( x ) = cx 2 + O ( x 3 ) .

texts.orig: Problem 2. Assume that the linearized operator ( -△ f + v, for example,) of a hamiltonian system is of pure point spectra, part of which is dense in some finite interval, and assume that the nonlinear part of the Hamiltonian is not of short rang (in partial differential equations defined in the whole space, for example). Are there any KAM tori for the system?

Are there any KAM tori for the system?
